1: 'Where has your beloved gone, O most beautiful of women?' — the daughters of Jerusalem, having heard the extended *wasf* of 5:10-16, are now won over: they want to help search. The title 'most beautiful of women' (*hayafa bannashim*) confirms the social recognition that was claimed in 1:8; the community's estimation of the beloved is changed by the beloved's own testimony about him. Their offer to search together reflects the community's role in supporting the love relationship.
2: 'My beloved has gone down to his garden, to the beds of spices, to graze in the gardens and gather lilies' — the woman, in the very act of answering the search-question, realizes: she knows where he is. The garden (*ganno*) is her; he has gone to her. The spice beds and lily-grazing return to the pastoral imagery of 2:1-2 and 4:13-14. The woman's sudden realization that she is the garden provides the transition to the mutual-possession formula of v. 3.
3: 'I am my beloved's and he is mine; he grazes among the lilies' — the refrain of mutual possession restated; this is the second of three occurrences (2:16; 6:3; 7:10). The subtle change from 2:16 ('my beloved is mine and I am his') to 6:3 ('I am my beloved's and he is mine') marks a shift of emphasis: in 2:16 she asserts her claim on him first; in 6:3 his claim on her comes first. The variation is not random but reflects the deepening of the relationship through the trials of the intervening chapters.
4: 'You are beautiful as Tirzah, lovely as Jerusalem, awe-inspiring as an army under banners' — Tirzah (*Tirtzah*, 'pleasantness') was the capital of the northern kingdom before Omri built Samaria (1 Kgs 15:33; 16:17-18, 23-24); it was presumably as famous for beauty as Jerusalem was for grandeur. To compare the beloved to both capitals is to say she embodies all of Israel's beauty in both its northern and southern forms. *Nidgalot* (as armies with banners) — the military standard (*degel*) again; the beloved's beauty is simultaneously lovely and formidable, inspiring both desire and awe.
5: 'Look away — your eyes overwhelm me' — *hirhivuni* (they have made me restless, disturbed me) from *rahav* (to overwhelm, to be turbulent); the same root as Rahab, the chaos-monster of the deep (Ps 89:10; Isa 51:9). The beloved's eyes cause the same disruption in the lover that the divine glory causes: an overwhelming, destabilizing experience. The echo of Ezekiel's vision language and the Song's use of overwhelming beauty suggest a consciousness that creaturely beauty can produce something analogous to the religious experience of the divine presence.
6: The description of teeth (repeating 4:2) and cheeks (v. 7, repeating 4:3) — the repetition of elements from the first *wasf* is a literary device signaling the continuing and unchanging beauty of the beloved. In oral poetry, the repetition of formulaic descriptions reinforced the beauty's consistency across time and occasion; the beloved is as beautiful in this moment as she was at the beginning of the *wasf*-sequence.
7: 'Your cheeks are like pomegranate halves behind your veil' — repeating 4:3; the veil (*tzamah*) appears again, creating the partial visibility that heightens the beauty. The pomegranate's blush through the veil is one of the Song's most evocative images — the fruit's color visible through the fabric, suggesting beauty barely concealed.
8: 'There may be sixty queens and eighty concubines, and maidens beyond counting' — the numbers scale hierarchically: queens (*melachot*, 60), concubines (*pilegashim*, 80), and unlimited maidens (*almot ein mispar*). The harem scale invokes the Solomonic court tradition (1 Kgs 11:3 reports 700 wives and 300 concubines, numbers that represent legendary excess); whether literal or hyperbolic, the comparison establishes the context: amid all possible women, the beloved is unique.
9: 'My dove, my flawless one, is unique — her mother's only daughter, treasured by the one who bore her' — *achat hi* (she is the one, the unique one): the superlative of singularity. 'Only daughter' (*yechidah le'immah*) recalls Psalm 22:20 ('my only life'; *yechidati*) and Judges 11:34 (Jephthah's only daughter). Being the mother's only daughter and her beloved (*barah*) implies both preciousness and vulnerability. The social validation that follows — queens and concubines praising her — inverts the usual hierarchy: those of higher formal status acknowledge the superiority of the beloved.
10: 'Who is this who appears like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, bright as the sun, awe-inspiring as an army with banners?' — the fourth appearance of the *nidgalot* (army with banners) formula (cf. v.4). The sequence dawn/moon/sun scales upward in luminosity: the dawn announces the day; the moon is the night's full light; the sun is the supreme light-source. In the ancient near east, the celestial bodies had patron deities (sun = Shamash; moon = Sin; Venus/dawn = Ishtar); the Song borrows their luminosity while emptying them of divine status, applying the cosmic comparisons to a human beloved.
11: 'I went down to the grove of walnut trees to look at the new growth in the valley' — *ginnat egoz* (walnut grove) was an agricultural feature of the Palestinian landscape; the inspection of new growth (*yonqot* = new shoots; *hanetzu hagenafim* = whether the vines have budded; *pitchu harimonim* = whether the pomegranates have bloomed) reflects the agricultural calendar's rhythms. The lover, having praised the beloved to the heights of cosmic imagery, is here shown in a mundane scene of agricultural inspection — the movement from cosmic praise to ordinary life.
12: 'Before I realized it, my longing had placed me beside my noble kinsman's chariot' — *ammi-nadib* is one of the Song's most contested phrases: a proper name ('Amminadib'), a compound noun ('my people's noble one'), or a reference to the beloved himself. The sense seems to be: the woman's longing (*nefesh*) transported her, before she knew it, to the presence of the beloved's chariot. The sudden transportation of the *nefesh* (the whole self, the deep desire) is a metaphor for how overwhelming longing relocates a person — she was in the walnut grove; suddenly, mentally/emotionally, she was beside him.
13: 'Come back, come back, O Shulamite' — the only occurrence of the name 'Shulamite' (*hashulammit*) in the Song. The most likely derivation is from Shunem (*Shunam*) in the Jezreel Valley (cf. Abishag the Shunammite, 1 Kgs 1:3; the great woman of Shunem, 2 Kgs 4:8). The feminine suffix gives a gentilic meaning: the woman from Shunem. Alternatively, *Shulammit* is the feminine form of *Shlomo* (Solomon) — a mirror-name, suggesting the beloved and the lover are counterparts of the same name. The 'dance between two companies' (*machol hamachanayim*) may be a processional or battle dance where two groups face each other.
